item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I was interested how fast can SCP-018 actually go. I whipped out a Python simulation assuming that the ball is 6 cm in diameter, and is made of solid rubber with a density of 1.2 kg/dm^3. I also used 0.47 drag coefficient taken from Wikipedia. The coefficient of restitution was 200% assuming that's what the article means. The ball was dropped from 1 meter assuming a perfectly vertical bounce which gives it the highest possible speed it can achieve.

The results are not nearly as crazy as is thought. The ball reaches a max velocity of around 250 km/h right after bouncing from the ground and reaches an altitude of about 120 meters. As far as the article goes it was written fairly realistic whether intentionally or not. The ball could very well be bouncing around for a few days before landing in a lake.

Speaking of the lake, water has almost 800 times the density of air and because it is incompressible it has a lot more drag for fast objects than if it was a gas as dense as water. Long story short, even at 250 km/h the ball stops almost instantly.

On another hand, the containment procedure seems to be a bit over the top. It doesn't take a gel or molten plastic to stop it and it would make a lot more sense to fill the tank with water which would be cheaper, more practical, and a lot safer for the containment crew.

Also, sorry for replying to a very old comment but I saw that no one seemed to take the time to actually find out what would happen and how dangerous actually is this SCP.

+ show block
– hide block

staff notice: please collapse images in the forums

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not coming to a store near me 😔
Also +1. This SCP was great!

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: There is no indication that this image has been released under Creative Commons. The original image dates back to RixPix of 1999, a defunct company that could not be found. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best toy known to mankind.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very basic concept, and has little to no mystery to it, but is still a good SCP because of its simplicity and somewhat creative concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been done… Many times in other fictions.

item.title: Re: Perpetual motion machine
item.content:encoded: yeah but infinite energy

item.title: Re: Perpetual motion machine
item.content:encoded: The problem there is it's not just Perpetual Motion, it's Perpetual Acceleration. Eventually it would go fast enough to wreck whatever machine they put it in and by then it would be going at seriously dangerous speeds. Like, world breaking speeds.

item.title: Perpetual motion machine
item.content:encoded: I would love to see this be used in a perpetual motion machine. Like I know about anonymity and all but that could just solve any and all energy problems (at least for the foundation) and it can count as containment. Also and more importantly… INFINITE POWER!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does Dr. █████████ have Overseer clearance? I presume not, because otherwise they would be referred to by an O5-# designation rather than a (redacted) name. If not, how do they know about SCP-006? Knowledge of SCP-006 and its properties are restricted to those with Overseer clearance.

Dr. █████████ had better watch their back…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the ball just keeps bouncing, it can somehow reach the speed of light and even go faster than that, but E=mc^2, therefor its mass, when it supasses the speed of light must be negative.

So the ball doesn´t obey the laws of physics, and someday we could use that tecnology from time travelling, because light makes time.

*mindblown*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one isn't bad but rather than the jokey ending, I think it would have been more interesting if the foundation used this for a perpetual energy machine, given that this object could provide that and the foundation would benefit greatly from free perpetual energy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: woah woah woah wouldn't it keep bouncing after hitting the water at such a quick velocity? Also a shit ton of lolfoundation at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's basically the ball from Men in Black?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really have a problem with the Researcher asking for permission to use SCP-018 this way, but being granted permission? Especially since the logic behind the object's 'practical' applications doesn't really make any sense? Taking a step would either crater the ground under your foot, assuming he's not understating the usefulness of the object, or break your leg with the recoil, and since, from what I can tell, the Foundation only has one ball available, it would require the Agent to be able to operate with one normal leg and one ridiculous super-bouncy leg.

And then there's the fact that the O5s okay'd it; even if this supersuit was a reasonable idea, it'd—well. The attached document is a pretty good best case scenario of what could happen if someone made a mistake in this suit. Worst case scenario, one of the more malicious/anti-Foundation Groups of Interest gets hold of the suit and uses it to break into a Foundation facility.

So that's a -1 from me, I guess. Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was only interesting until I read the blurb at the end with the researcher wantig to combine various SCPs for his armor, and seeing how excited he was. It was hilarious. I can imagine people like that giving the higher-ups huge headaches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally like the Series 3, they somehow can still make well articles (I mean the successful ones) when they know that there is almost no inspiration anymore. (I think they brainstorm on forums)

Additional information;

+ show block
– hide block

Agree but I think the Series 1 has better ideas but also weak executed articles, now from when I joined the site on I relised that more SCPs from the 3rd series has been deleted due too much downvotes, note that people are relizing that this site may not stand full of crappy SCPs, take a look at the articles in Series 1, like now I only know 2 re-written SCPs due there previous versions were downvoted too much. I think in the future more SCPs in Series 1 are going to be deleted. (as example; SCP-012)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man…

I've been re-reading Series 1 and reflecting on how good so many of them actually are minus a few fuckups. People keep saying Series 1 hasn't aged well, and I've been thinking in response "actually, it's more our circa 2013 standards that aren't aging well" (both the confusion of tropes with quality and the refusal to clean anything up ever).

This is not such a situation.

There's some stuff worth preserving in here, and I actually quite like some of the old-style stuff like the armor suits, but overall this is pretty stupid and ripping off Flubber in a rather dull way. Also lolFoundaton with the 006 reference.

+1 changed to -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Document 018-04 needs to be edited. Due to the physics that the super ball was never documented to defy, if anyone were to kick something with that armor, either their legs with break or they'll dislocate it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The conspicuous Flubber references, lolFoundation shenanigans and scientific goofs killed this for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd just like to take a minute to admire this little thing. This is easily one of my favorite skips, if only because it is just such a cool idea. It really represents the Foundation's "Well here's something that defies the laws of physics in a particular way, let's try to exploit it as much as possible" attitude, and for such a simple concept, it's displayed remarkably well, even considering it's an oldie. Also, correct me if I'm wrong, but isn't this just the perfect Wondertainment object? I dunno, guys, I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the main idea, but the end gives me some lolFoundation vibe, which, as far as I know, is heavily frowned upon curently. Maybe some change in tone would be enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Started off super awesome, ended up not so good. I love the object. Yes, I get the flubber reference, but I think it's sufficiently distinct from flubber as to be its own thing.
However, the idea of an agent, just an agent, not a researcher or doctor or director, knowing what 006 is just seems flatly absurd to me and kills the suspension of disbelief. If he had asked for a pill of 500 or some other restorative, it would be an unadulterated upvote. As it stands, the good and bad cancel out. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So much padding towards the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, yeah. It was bouncing inside the lake for several days. That hardly takes away from any other issue that I pointed out though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is necro, but for the record, it wasn't bouncing around for several days. It was only bouncing around for a few minutes, but when it fell into the lake it took a couple days for it to come to rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sold exclusively at Vat19.com

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't like this article in its current form. I realize that it's one of the ancient ones, but it's showing its age a bit too well. I also realize that it's probably too high in its rating for these ideas to take hold/problems to be addressed. However, I feel that it could be improved if some alterations were made to it, nonetheless.

show review
hide review

The first thing to bug me about this (and it's ultimately a small thing with an easy fix) was that I thought it could cause problems with the CCL if we used licensed/copyrighted products or logos in our skips or tales. Should we not blackbox out the Wham-O name?

Looking at the containment procedures, I'm a bit bothered by them. So we have it in a harness (made of undisclosed materials) inside a heavily padded, 1m^3 box. I feel it would be best, in the interest of containment, to state what kind of padding is necessary, for the in-universe maintenance crews to have a swift and easy guide.

Also, I'd be kind of interested in seeing what materials the author could come up with to counter the kinetic amplification abilities, since all this thing needs is a light poke to become THE BOUNCY BALL OF DOOM!!!!TM

Now, I can also understand why it needs to be stored in a highly-viscous material, because of its kinetic energy amplification attributes, but the use of the term 'goo' in an official document is kind of annoying. Further, this thing is submerged in molten plastic. I'm only going off of Wikipedia here, but that's somewhere in the 250 degrees Fahrenheit range, which would make it kind of difficult for any containment teams to access or restore the item to its containment box.

Nevermind the displacement issues with a ginormous pool of liquid plastic in a room with very specific measurements that doesn't appear to leave any room for error or someone else to get inside it. Also, how is the temperature maintained to keep the plastic molten? That's never addressed.

Further, though I have certainly been guilty of it, this is "one (1)" of my pet peeves, and it's used throughout the article inconsistently.

At first thought to be a pleasant child's toy,

This tonally bugs the crap out of me.

The ball soon became a dangerous projectile,

This just doesn't feel like how a scientific document would address that particular situation.

It came to a rest after several days in the nearby lake of ████████, and was retrieved by SCP personnel.

Okay, I'm only taking a basic physics course at the moment, but that just screams out to me as weird. Several days of uncontained bouncing around should have this thing going at Ludicrous Speed, if not, at the very least, escape velocity, as each bounce would double its speed.

Just assuming that it was going from a starting speed of, say, ten meters a second, within ten bounces (that it likely surpassed within the first minute or so, so it was likely 20-30 times that, I'm just trying to illustrate my point) it would be going something like five or ten kilometers per second, and I have a hard time believing a lake would be enough to stop it.

Further, as I mentioned earlier, this thing could be sent on a rampage with a light poke. How in the hell did the Foundation remove it from the lake, much less transport it to a containment site?

Due to the speed of the object, and the total surprise by its victims, no cover-up story was required or initiated.

Bull. Shit. The Foundation wouldn't just leave a story out about a bouncy ball rampaging through a town for just shy of a week. They would find some plausible explanation and flaunt it everywhere they could.

The first letter I find laughable at best, and completely flying in the face of my headcanon on the Foundation. The Foundation would never authorize turning one of their agents into Speedball to contain objects/creatures, especially when, as the letters showed, having this thing on one boot by itself will just likely lead to the poor schmuck being dragged around by his leg and getting bashed on and through everything in between himself and the target. Doubly so when the guy's best defense of his proposal to, and I cannot stress this enough, AN O5 BOARD MEMBER is effectively saying "You know you can trust me, brah, we go way back."

The second letter bugs me for a few reasons. First, because the O5 didn't laugh as he read the letter, before giving a calm, measured, and professionally worded reply of "There is no way in hell I'm going to authorize that", and instead allows the researcher to go through with the idea, getting one of their best operatives if not outright killed, then certainly put on the early retirement list.

Then it outright states that the agent contained some beastie or another down in the amazon by dismembering it, which again flies against my headcanon. Further, it's quite difficult to believe that, even if this had been a test run, the O5 would be willing to allow testing/development to continue with the super-suit, considering the injuries obtained by the poor bastard getting launched into midair. I also find that (Highly armed/armored dude getting bitchslapped by a bouncy ball strapped to his boot a mile into the air) to be a situation the foundation would need to cover up, no mention of which was made.

Also, there is only one of these balls in containment mentioned, and if I remember the earlier part of the article correctly, there was no success in duplicating or deciphering how the ball worked in order to replicate its effects. How would this become common use, aside from the rather ridiculous image I have in my head of a strike team passing the ball back and forth in midair?

And finally, the last letter is the most annoying part of this. The Foundation would not allow mass-cross-contamination of different SCP items just to create a super soldier.

TL;DR version, this is woefully under-detailed and flies against my headcanon on how the Foundation operates, as well as my understanding of basic physics, and not in a good way. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The requisition request and commentary at the end sold me.
Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's the recovery story for this?

"Sir, did you see that red blur a moment ago?"

"Yes, it was headed North-North-West at 349 degrees, and as its last bounce was a shallow angle, its trajectory resembled the quadratic curve of y=-1/11x2 +2.33x+2, assuming my perspective at x=-4, in meters."

"So towards the lake?"

"Yeah. Who did you say you were?"

"I'm with the Unusual Incident Unit of the FBI. Please file a report with them, err, us."

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: Changed to Euclid. Progress.

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: Go for Euclid.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: Oy, another slash class. Honestly, how can a single item be both Safe and Euclid? Either there's a significant chance of it breaching containment if you leave it alone, or there isn't. I wanna make this just Euclid, or just Safe, or hell just Embla if there's a persuasive argument for it— whatever, just not a dual-class. Just need some modly/adminly assent before I pull that trigger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not just a super ball.

A super duper ball.

item.title: Couldn't resist...
item.content:encoded: Do not taunt happy fun ball

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the addenda in this one. It shows that there's plenty of crazy bastards in the foundation, just like us, going "Ooh, so much shiny stuff! Let's play with it!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My impression is that it delivers 200% of the effort initially delivered, but it can still be slowed by friction and surfaces which are not perfectly hard. Thus, why it gets stopped by water and the gel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only if every bounce it hit a good reflective surface, and it was always angled to bounce UP on a hit…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright. I don't wanna field what's probably an obvious question here, but, uh… wouldn't a ball that goes twice as high every time it bounces shoot off into space and be lost forever after a couple of hours?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Access to it. Knowledge about it is still restricted but less so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't SCP-006 ultra-super top secret and only O5s know the real story on it? Why does this researcher know enough about it to casually suggest using some as a restorative?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, you'd think they'd make a series of movies about it, and perhaps later on have a crappy remake.

One of the few instances where I actually like a SCP obviously inspired by a movie.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Not only that, but the idea of a rubber polymer which amplifies its own kinetic energy by double is, frankly, really cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I give this a plus one, just because i love super balls so much, and am glad they made it into SCP.
